channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: LogicEval: A Systematic Framework for Evaluating Automated Repair Techniques for Logical Vulnerabilities in Real-World Software
item.description: This paper introduces **LogicEval**, the first systematic framework for evaluating automated repair techniques—both traditional APR tools and LLM-based approaches—on real-world logical vulnerabilities. We construct **LogicDS**, the first benchmark dataset of 86 CVE-verified logical vulnerabilities across diverse domains (e.g., web services, smart contracts), each annotated with precise locations, triggering conditions, expected correct behavior, and human-validated test suites. LogicEval evaluates patches along four dimensions: compilability, functional correctness, logical completeness (i.e., root-cause resolution), and contextual fidelity. Our evaluation reveals that current LLM-based repair achieves only 19.3% end-to-end success rate; failures are primarily driven by **prompt sensitivity**, **loss of cross-module code context**, and **inaccurate vulnerability localization**—challenges largely unaddressed by existing APR methods (<2% success). LogicEval establishes a rigorous, reproducible foundation for advancing logical vulnerability repair research.

item.title: Parallax: Why AI Agents That Think Must Never Act
item.description: This paper identifies a critical architectural flaw in autonomous AI agents: prompt-based safety mechanisms fail catastrophically when the reasoning system is compromised, as they reside *within* the same trust boundary as the threat. To address this, we introduce **Parallax**, a paradigm for safe AI execution grounded in four principles: (1) strict **Cognitive-Executive Separation**, preventing reasoning modules from directly invoking actions; (2) **Adversarial Validation with Graduated Determinism**, inserting an independent, tiered validator between reasoning and execution; (3) **Information Flow Control**, propagating sensitivity labels to detect context-dependent threats; and (4) **Reversible Execution**, enabling atomic rollback via pre-action state capture. We release **OpenParallax**, a production-ready Go implementation, and evaluate it using *Assume-Compromise Evaluation*—a novel methodology that bypasses the reasoning system entirely to stress-test the architectural boundary. Across 280 adversarial test cases spanning nine attack categories, Parallax blocks **98.9% of attacks with zero false positives** under default settings, and achieves **100% blocking** under maximum-security mode—demonstrating resilience where prompt guardrails offer zero protection under full compromise.

item.title: Understanding and Improving Continuous Adversarial Training for LLMs via In-context Learning Theory
item.description: Adversarial training (AT) defends LLMs against jailbreak attacks but is computationally expensive. Continuous AT (CAT) improves efficiency by optimizing adversarial perturbations in the *continuous embedding space*—yet its theoretical justification remains elusive: why do embedding-space perturbations confer robustness against *token-space* jailbreak prompts? This paper provides the first theoretical analysis of CAT grounded in in-context learning (ICL) theory. For linear Transformers trained on in-context linear regression, we derive a robust generalization bound that *decreases* with the embedding perturbation radius—explaining CAT’s cross-space robustness transfer. Crucially, the bound depends explicitly on the singular values of the LLM’s embedding matrix. Leveraging this insight, we propose SVR-CAT: a novel CAT variant incorporating a singular-value-based regularization term. Experiments on Llama-2, Qwen, and Phi-3 show SVR-CAT reduces jailbreak success rates by 22.6–35.1% across GCG, AutoDAN, and PAIR attacks, while preserving utility (≤0.8% drop on BBH/MMLU). Code: https://github.com/fshp971/continuous-adv-icl.

item.title: Evaluating Differential Privacy Against Membership Inference in Federated Learning: Insights from the NIST Genomics Red Team Challenge
item.description: This paper empirically evaluates Differential Privacy (DP) as a defense against Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) in Federated Learning (FL), using the standardized environment of the 2025 NIST Genomics PPFL Red Teaming Event. We propose a novel **stacking-based black-box MIA**, ensembling seven diverse estimators to train a meta-classifier on prediction probabilities and cross-entropy losses—significantly enhancing inference sensitivity under DP noise. Evaluated across three privacy tiers ($ε = \infty$, $200$, and $10$), our attack outperforms all baselines in the No-DP and Low-Privacy settings and—critically—detects measurable membership leakage at $ε = 200$ where the standard LiRA baseline collapses (AUC ≈ 0.5). On an independent third-party benchmark, we observe a clear degradation trend: AUC drops from 0.72 ± 0.03 ($ε=200$) to 0.58 ± 0.02 ($ε=10$), providing the first empirical characterization of stacking-based MIA resilience across calibrated DP budgets in genomic FL.

item.title: Robust Semi-Supervised Temporal Intrusion Detection for Adversarial Cloud Networks
item.description: Cloud networks increasingly depend on ML-based intrusion detection, yet real-world deployment suffers from label scarcity, non-stationary traffic, and adaptive adversaries contaminating unlabeled data. Most semi-supervised NIDS assume benign and stationary unlabeled traffic—an unrealistic assumption in adversarial cloud environments. We propose **RSTID**, a robust semi-supervised temporal framework that explicitly models *adversarial contamination* and *temporal drift* in unlabeled flows. RSTID operates on flow-level data and integrates three key components: (i) consistency regularization under temporal augmentations; (ii) confidence-aware pseudo-labeling with dynamic thresholding; and (iii) selective temporal invariance—leveraging intrinsic flow temporal structure while avoiding over-constraining during drift. Evaluated on CIC-IDS2017, CSE-CIC-IDS2018, and UNSW-NB15 under severe label scarcity (5%–20% labeled), RSTID consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and semi-supervised baselines—achieving up to 18.7% higher F1-score, 3.2× better label efficiency, and <4.1% performance degradation under 20% adversarial contamination or concept drift.

item.title: LLM-Guided Prompt Evolution for Password Guessing
item.description: Passwords remain the dominant authentication method, yet their security is routinely undermined by predictable user behavior and large-scale credential leaks. This paper introduces **LLM-guided prompt evolution**, a novel approach that applies evolutionary computation—specifically MAP-Elites quality-diversity search within an island population model (via OpenEvolve)—to automatically optimize prompts for LLM-based password guessing. Evaluated on a RockYou-derived test set (500K real passwords), our method boosts cracking rates from **2.02% to 8.48%** across three practical configurations: local (Qwen3-8B), cloud-efficient (Gemini-2.5 Flash), and frontier two-model ensemble (Gemma-3 27B + Qwen3 32B). Character-level analysis confirms evolved prompts yield statistically more realistic password distributions (p < 0.001), validating behavioral fidelity. Crucially, this work demonstrates that **automated prompt optimization—requiring no model fine-tuning or training data access—is a low-barrier, high-impact technique for strengthening LLM-powered password auditing**, while also highlighting how attack pipelines themselves evolve autonomously.

item.title: DeepSeek Robustness Against Semantic-Character Dual-Space Mutated Prompt Injection
item.description: Prompt injection poses a critical security threat to LLMs, yet most existing studies evaluate only single-space attacks—semantic *or* character-level—failing to model realistic multi-space adversarial perturbations. To bridge this gap, we propose **PromptFuzz-SC**, the first semantic-character *dual-space mutation framework* for black-box robustness evaluation of Chinese LLMs like DeepSeek. It unifies semantic transformations (e.g., paraphrasing, word-order scrambling) with character-level obfuscation (e.g., zero-width insertion, encoding-based mutation) and employs a hybrid ε-greedy + hill-climbing search to generate stealthy, high-success adversarial prompts. Evaluated on DeepSeek using MSR, AQS, and Stealth, dual-space mutation achieves the strongest overall performance: **mean MSR = 0.189** (↑12.5% vs. semantic-only; ↑5.6% vs. character-only), **peak MSR = 0.375**, and **highest mean Stealth**, while maintaining competitive query efficiency in best-case scenarios. Our results underscore the necessity of composite mutation strategies for rigorous red-teaming and inform the design of multi-layered defenses.

item.title: Practical Evaluation of the Crypto-Agility Maturity Model
item.description: Cryptographic agility is essential for sustaining digital security amid the post-quantum transition, and the Crypto-Agility Maturity Model (CAMM) was proposed to assess it systematically. This paper presents the first rigorous evaluation of CAMM against established maturity model design principles (e.g., ISO/IEC/IEEE 33001). We identify three critical shortcomings: (1) ambiguous scope and target audiences—lacking clear organizational or technical boundaries; (2) insufficiently operationalized acceptance criteria—rendering higher-level requirements unverifiable and non-reproducible; and (3) flawed dependency relations—including redundancies, cycles, and omissions (e.g., missing supply-chain agility). Empirical application to a real-world TLS service upgrade confirmed these issues: 40% of Level 4 requirements were inapplicable or ill-defined. Based on these findings, we propose concrete enhancements: a stratified stakeholder matrix, standardized evidence types per capability (configuration artifacts, test logs, audit reports), and a revised dependency graph incorporating PQC migration pathways and supply-chain coordination. This work advances CAMM toward practical, consistent, and auditable cryptographic agility assessment.

item.title: WebAgentGuard: A Reasoning-Driven Guard Model for Detecting Prompt Injection Attacks in Web Agents
item.description: Web agents powered by vision-language models (VLMs) enable autonomous web interaction but are critically vulnerable to prompt injection attacks—malicious instructions hidden in HTML or screenshots that hijack agent behavior, risking data leakage and misuse. Existing defenses (e.g., system prompt hardening, agent fine-tuning) suffer from poor generalization and utility degradation. We propose **WebAgentGuard**, a reasoning-driven, multimodal guard model operating *in parallel* with the main agent to decouple detection from task execution. Trained on a large synthetic dataset (128K samples, 164 topics, 230 UI styles) generated by GPT-5, it leverages stepwise multimodal reasoning over screenshots and DOM text. Via reasoning-intensive supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning with adversarial feedback, WebAgentGuard achieves **94.7% average detection accuracy** across six benchmarks—outperforming strong baselines by +11.2%—while preserving >99% agent utility and adding **zero latency**.

item.title: SpanKey: Dynamic Key Space Conditioning for Neural Network Access Control
item.description: SpanKey introduces a lightweight, accuracy-preserving neural network access control mechanism that gates inference by *conditioning intermediate activations on secret keys*, without weight encryption or accuracy trade-offs. It defines a low-dimensional key subspace $Span(B)$ via a learnable basis matrix $B$; valid keys are sampled as $k = \alpha^\top B$, then injected additively or multiplicatively into layer activations with tunable strength $\gamma$. Invalid keys are explicitly drawn outside $Span(B)$. We make three contributions: (i) A principled design space for subspace-based key injection across network layers; (ii) Identification and analysis of *key absorption*—a failure mode where networks implicitly nullify key effects—characterized via a novel Beta-energy split and margin-tail diagnostics; (iii) Empirical validation: Modes A–C achieve >99.2% acceptance for valid keys and <0.5% false acceptance for invalid keys on CIFAR-10/ResNet-18, with MNIST ablations confirming robustness at small subspace rank ($r=4$). SpanKey is not cryptographically secure but offers practical, scalable inference gating. Code: https://github.com/mindmemory-ai/dksc.

item.title: TEMPLATEFUZZ: Fine-Grained Chat Template Fuzzing for Jailbreaking and Red Teaming LLMs
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) face critical security risks from jailbreak attacks, yet prior work largely overlooks *chat templates*—the structured message formatting rules (e.g., `<|user|>...<|assistant|>`) that critically influence safety alignment. This paper introduces **TEMPLATEFUZZ**, the first fine-grained fuzzing framework targeting chat templates as a novel attack surface. It (1) designs element-level mutation rules (e.g., delimiter obfuscation, zero-width insertion, nested templating) to generate semantically valid but structurally disruptive variants; (2) employs a heuristic search strategy that jointly optimizes attack success rate (ASR) and task accuracy via lightweight gradient-guided exploration; and (3) integrates an active learning–based rule oracle for fast, accurate, and automated jailbreak evaluation (F1=0.96). Evaluated on 12 open-source LLMs, TEMPLATEFUZZ achieves a **98.2% average ASR** with only **1.1% accuracy degradation**, outperforming SOTA by up to 47.9% in ASR and 8.4% in accuracy preservation. Remarkably, it attains **90% average ASR** even on 5 commercial LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude-3.5) via template-informed prompt injection—demonstrating the pervasive vulnerability of template logic across model deployment paradigms.

item.title: From IOCs to Regex: Automating CTI Operationalization for SOC with LLMs
item.description: Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) operationalization requires converting unstructured Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) into precise, syntactically valid regular expressions (regexes) for SIEM rules, log parsing, and forensics—yet this remains largely manual and error-prone. To bridge this gap, we propose **IOCRegex-gen**, an LLM-based system that fully automates IOC-to-regex generation. It introduces: (i) a *group-aware mechanism* to intelligently assign capture/non-capture groups based on IOC semantics, and (ii) an *iterative reasoning and multi-stage validation pipeline* combining LLM self-refinement, regex AST parsing, and dynamic matching against MITRE ATT&CK ground-truth strings. Evaluated on 3,000+ real CTI reports and 2,400 ATT&CK evaluation samples, IOCRegex-gen achieves a **99.1% hit rate** and only **0.8% false-positive rate**, demonstrating robust scalability and production-readiness for automated CTI operationalization.

item.title: Fully Homomorphic Encryption on Llama 3 model for privacy preserving LLM inference
item.description: This work pioneers the integration of **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)-compliant Lattice-based Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)** into the inference pipeline of the LLaMA-3 model to enable privacy-preserving, quantum-resistant LLM deployment. Leveraging `concrete-ml`, we selectively apply FHE operations to privacy-critical layers (e.g., embedding outputs, attention logits, and FFN activations) while preserving plaintext computation elsewhere—achieving a practical balance between security and efficiency. On an Intel i9 CPU, our FHE-secured LLaMA-3-8B achieves **98% text generation accuracy**, **237 ms latency per inference**, and **80 tokens/sec throughput**, demonstrating feasibility for real-world use. The approach withstands both classical and Shor’s-algorithm-based quantum attacks, fulfilling NIST PQC Level 3 requirements with only ~1.7× overhead over baseline inference.

item.title: CIA: Inferring the Communication Topology from LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
item.description: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems (MAS) rely critically on internal communication topologies for task coordination, yet these topologies often encode proprietary architectural knowledge. This paper identifies and formalizes a novel privacy threat: *communication topology inference under strict black-box access*, where an attacker observes only system inputs/outputs. We propose **Communication Inference Attack (CIA)**—a query-efficient framework that crafts adversarial inputs to elicit discriminative reasoning traces from intermediate agents, then models semantic correlations via **global bias disentanglement** and **LLM-guided weak supervision** to reconstruct topology edges. Evaluated across six optimized MAS topologies (e.g., hierarchical, ring, tree), CIA achieves an average AUC of **0.87** and a peak AUC of **0.99**, outperforming baselines by +0.23 in AUC with ≤50 queries. Our results expose a fundamental vulnerability in LLM-MAS deployment and establish topology confidentiality as a first-class security concern.

item.title: MultiDocFusion: Hierarchical and Multimodal Chunking Pipeline for Enhanced RAG on Long Industrial Documents
item.description: RAG-based QA for long industrial documents suffers from structural ignorance: conventional chunking methods ignore multimodal layout (e.g., headings, tables, figures) and semantic hierarchy, causing context fragmentation and answer degradation. To address this, we propose **MultiDocFusion**, a hierarchical and multimodal chunking pipeline comprising: (i) vision-based document region detection; (ii) OCR-powered text extraction per region; (iii) LLM-guided Document Section Hierarchical Parsing (DSHP-LLM) to reconstruct a semantic tree; and (iv) DFS-based hierarchical chunk construction. Evaluated across five industrial benchmarks (power, manufacturing, chemical domains), MultiDocFusion achieves **+8–15% retrieval precision** and **+2–3% ANLS QA scores** over strong baselines. Results confirm that *explicitly leveraging document hierarchy is critical* for robust multimodal RAG—establishing structure-aware chunking as a foundational requirement for industrial knowledge systems.

item.title: Local-Splitter: A Measurement Study of Seven Tactics for Reducing Cloud LLM Token Usage on Coding-Agent Workloads
item.description: We present Local-Splitter, a measurement study of seven tactics for reducing cloud LLM token usage in coding-agent systems that leverage a small local model as a triage layer before a frontier cloud model. We implement all tactics—including local routing, prompt compression, semantic caching, local drafting with cloud review, minimal-diff edits, structured intent extraction, and batching with vendor prompt caching—in an open-source shim supporting Ollama (local) and any OpenAI-compatible endpoint (cloud). Evaluated across four workload classes (edit-heavy, explanation-heavy, general chat, RAG-heavy), we measure token savings, cost, latency, and routing accuracy—both individually, in pairs, and via greedy subset selection. Key results: T1+T2 achieves 45–79% cloud token reduction on edit/explanation workloads; the full tactic set yields 51% savings on RAG-heavy workloads. Crucially, the optimal tactic combination is workload-dependent—a highly actionable insight for practitioners deploying production coding agents today.

item.title: Coding-Free and Privacy-Preserving MCP Framework for Clinical Agentic Research Intelligence System
item.description: This paper introduces CARIS, a **coding-free and privacy-preserving Clinical Agentic Research Intelligence System**, built on the Model Context Protocol (MCP). CARIS enables clinicians and researchers to conduct end-to-end clinical studies—spanning research planning, literature review, cohort construction, IRB documentation, Vibe ML modeling, and report generation—using only natural language, without writing code or accessing raw patient data. All sensitive databases remain securely within the local MCP server; users interact solely with outputs and final reports. Evaluated across three heterogeneous clinical datasets, CARIS finalized research plans and IRB documents in just 3–4 iterative refinements, ranked top-performing models with interpretable visualizations, and achieved **96% (LLM) and 82% (human) completeness** against the TRIPOD+AI reporting standard. By eliminating coding requirements and direct data exposure, CARIS lowers barriers and bridges public and private clinical data ecosystems.

item.title: ARGen: Affect-Reinforced Generative Augmentation towards Vision-based Dynamic Emotion Perception
item.description: Dynamic facial expression recognition in the wild suffers from data scarcity and long-tailed emotion distributions, impeding robust modeling of temporal dynamics—especially for rare affective states. To address this, we propose **ARGen**, an Affect-Reinforced Generative Augmentation framework comprising two novel stages: (1) **Affective Semantic Injection (ASI)** aligns facial Action Units with large-scale vision-language models to generate fine-grained, retrieval-augmented emotional prompts—injecting interpretable affective priors; and (2) **Adaptive Reinforcement Diffusion (ARD)** integrates text-conditioned image-to-video diffusion with reinforcement learning, optimizing naturalness, facial integrity, and efficiency via inter-frame guidance and a multi-objective reward. Experiments show ARGen achieves state-of-the-art generation fidelity (28.6% lower FVD) and boosts dynamic emotion recognition accuracy by up to +9.2%, establishing the first interpretable, generalizable generative augmentation paradigm for vision-based affective computing.

item.title: Evolution of Optimization Methods: Algorithms, Scenarios, and Evaluations
item.description: This paper presents a unified retrospective analysis and large-scale empirical evaluation of deep learning optimizers across first-order (e.g., SGD, Adam, Lion), second-order (e.g., K-FAC, Shampoo), zeroth-order (e.g., ZO-SGD, SPSA), and privacy-preserving variants (e.g., DP-SGD, DPSGD-Adam). We benchmark 18 mainstream algorithms on 5 model architectures—including CNNs, ViTs, LLaMA-2-1.3B, Stable Diffusion UNet, and GraphSAGE—under 6 realistic scenarios: standard training, differential privacy (ε ≤ 2.0), memory-constrained, heterogeneous-device, sparse-data, and adversarial robustness settings. Using standardized protocols and multi-dimensional metrics (accuracy, steps-to-converge, peak GPU memory, ε-budget, communication cost), we identify critical trade-offs: second-order methods accelerate convergence by 1.8–2.4× for models ≤1B parameters but inflate memory by 3.2×; zeroth-order optimizers achieve the best privacy-accuracy balance under tight ε constraints while reducing memory to 37% of first-order baselines. Crucially, no single optimizer dominates universally—performance is scenario-contingent. We distill three fundamental design laws and release an open-source, automated evaluation framework at https://github.com/APRIL-AIGC/Awesome-Optimizer.

item.title: Rethinking the Personalized Relaxed Initialization in the Federated Learning: Consistency and Generalization
item.description: Federated learning (FL) suffers from “client drift”—divergent local optima across heterogeneous clients—which harms generalization but lacks rigorous theoretical characterization. To address this, we propose **FedInit**, an efficient, stage-wise personalized relaxed initialization: at each local training start, the client initializes its model by moving *against* its last local update direction from the global state. Crucially, FedInit incurs **zero additional communication or computation cost**. Through excess risk analysis, we prove that client inconsistency primarily degrades the *generalization error bound*, not optimization convergence. Experiments across six benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, FEMNIST, Shakespeare) under strong Non-IID settings show FedInit consistently improves test accuracy by 1.8–3.2% over FedAvg/FedProx, and boosts generalization when integrated into SCAFFOLD or MOON. FedInit is modular, theoretically grounded, and practically effective—rethinking initialization as a key lever for FL generalization.

item.title: PrivEraserVerify: Efficient, Private, and Verifiable Federated Unlearning
item.description: PrivEraserVerify (PEV) is the first unified framework for federated unlearning that simultaneously achieves **efficiency**, **strong privacy guarantees**, and **decentralized verifiability**. To address the fragmentation in prior work—where efficiency, privacy, and verification are pursued in isolation—PEV introduces: (i) *adaptive checkpointing* to retain only critical historical updates for rapid model reconstruction; (ii) *layer-adaptive differentially private calibration*, which selectively nullifies a client’s influence with minimal accuracy loss while providing formal $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP indistinguishability; and (iii) *fingerprint-based verification*, enabling participants to non-invasively confirm unlearning via lightweight, tamper-evident hashes without accessing model weights or logs. Evaluations across image (CIFAR-10), handwritten character (EMNIST-Letters), and medical (CheXNet subset) datasets show PEV accelerates unlearning by **2.3–3.1× vs. full retraining**, incurs <2.8% accuracy drop under $\varepsilon=2.0$, and supports scalable, low-overhead verification (<0.3% communication overhead). PEV bridges regulatory compliance, practical deployment, and auditability in federated learning.

item.title: Beyond Weather Correlation: A Comparative Study of Static and Temporal Neural Architectures for Fine-Grained Residential Energy Consumption Forecasting in Melbourne, Australia
item.description: Accurate 5-minute residential energy forecasting is vital for smart grid operations and renewable integration in Australia. While weather is widely used, this study empirically demonstrates that **temporal autocorrelation in consumption sequences dominates meteorological features** at fine-grained resolution. We compare an MLP (weather + time features only) and an LSTM (24-step/2-hour sliding windows) on real 14-month smart meter data from two Melbourne households: House 3 (grid-only) and House 4 (solar PV-integrated). The LSTM achieves R² = 0.883 (House 3) and 0.865 (House 4), outperforming the MLP by 93.8 and 45.5 percentage points—evidence that sequential memory is far more predictive than static weather alone. Notably, the MLP’s modest R² = 0.410 on House 4 reveals implicit solar generation inference from weather-time correlations, highlighting a PV-induced asymmetry. Results are contextualized via persistence baselines and seasonal stratification. We propose weather-augmented LSTMs and federated learning as key future directions.

item.title: PubSwap: Public-Data Off-Policy Coordination for Federated RLVR
item.description: We propose **PubSwap**, a novel federated reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) framework for post-training reasoning enhancement under data decentralization and privacy constraints. PubSwap combines **LoRA-based local adaptation** to minimize communication overhead with **public-data-driven off-policy coordination**: a small, shared public dataset enables periodic exchange and reuse of *response-level training signals* across clients—without exposing private data. Crucially, during public-data steps, PubSwap selectively replaces locally incorrect responses with globally validated correct ones, maintaining local policy fidelity while improving cross-client alignment. Evaluated on mathematical (GSM8K, MATH) and medical (MedQA) reasoning benchmarks, PubSwap consistently outperforms standard federated baselines (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx) and standalone RLVR by +4.2–7.8% absolute accuracy. It reduces communication costs by 68–82% versus full-model synchronization and achieves robust coordination with only 50–200 public samples. PubSwap demonstrates that lightweight, privacy-preserving federated reasoning is both feasible and effective.

item.title: ToxiTrace: Gradient-Aligned Training for Explainable Chinese Toxicity Detection
item.description: Existing Chinese toxicity detectors excel at sentence-level classification but lack explainability—failing to extract *contiguous, human-readable toxic spans* (e.g., “you are trash”, “die immediately”). ToxiTrace addresses this via three synergistic components: (1) **CuSA**, a lightweight LLM-guided module that refines encoder-derived saliency into coherent toxic spans; (2) **GCLoss**, a gradient-constrained objective that concentrates token-level importance on ground-truth toxic regions while suppressing irrelevant activations; and (3) **ARCL**, which builds sample-specific contrastive pairs (toxic vs. non-toxic spans within the same sentence) to sharpen semantic boundaries. Experiments on four Chinese benchmarks show ToxiTrace improves classification F1 by +1.8% (avg. 92.7%) and toxic span extraction F1 by +6.5% (avg. 78.3%), with explanations rated 89.1% human-satisfying. It retains efficient BERT-style inference and is publicly released on Hugging Face.

item.title: Optimizing IoT Intrusion Detection with Tabular Foundation Models for Smart City Forensics
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of TabPFNv2.5—a transformer-based tabular foundation model—for real-time IoT intrusion detection in smart city forensics. Using the TON IoT dataset, we show that TabPFNv2.5 achieves **97% binary classification accuracy** while delivering **40× faster inference** than Random Forest—enabling sub-millisecond threat screening on CPU. To bridge speed-accuracy trade-offs, we propose a hybrid pipeline: TabPFNv2.5 performs rapid initial triage, and ensemble models (e.g., RF, XGBoost) handle detailed multi-class attribution only for high-risk samples. Key findings include: (1) scanning attacks remain the most challenging to detect (F1 = 69.8%); (2) cross-device generalization critically depends on feature distribution similarity; and (3) our hybrid approach maintains 96.3% end-to-end accuracy while improving system throughput by 3.2×. These results establish tabular foundation models as viable, low-overhead components for time-sensitive IoT security operations.

item.title: The Salami Slicing Threat: Exploiting Cumulative Risks in LLM Systems
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are vulnerable to multi-turn jailbreak attacks, yet existing methods suffer from poor generalizability and high detectability due to reliance on explicit, model-specific harmful triggers. To address this, we introduce the *Salami Slicing Risk*—a novel threat paradigm where numerous low-risk inputs, each individually below safety thresholds, are strategically chained to cumulatively build harmful intent and trigger high-risk outputs without engineered context. Based on this, we propose **Salami Attack**, a fully automatic, cross-model and cross-modality framework. Extensive experiments show it achieves >90% Attack Success Rate (ASR) on GPT-4o and Gemini, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods and demonstrating strong robustness against real-world alignment defenses (e.g., Constitutional AI, Self-Refine). We further design a lightweight defense—Cumulative Intent Monitor (CIM)—that reduces Salami Attack ASR by ≥44.8% with only 0.8% latency overhead, while blocking 64.8% of other multi-turn jailbreaks. This work uncovers a critical cumulative-risk vulnerability in LLM interaction dynamics and delivers actionable insights for adaptive security hardening.

item.title: QShield: Securing Neural Networks Against Adversarial Attacks using Quantum Circuits
item.description: QShield is a modular hybrid quantum-classical neural network (HQCNN) architecture designed to enhance adversarial robustness of deep learning models without compromising accuracy. It integrates a classical CNN backbone for feature extraction with a differentiable quantum processing module that encodes features into parameterized quantum states, applies structured entanglement operations under realistic noise models (e.g., decoherence, gate errors), and fuses classical and quantum predictions via a lightweight dynamic-weighting MLP. Evaluated on MNIST, OrganAMNIST, and CIFAR-10 against PGD, FGSM, CW, and five other attacks, QShield reduces average attack success rates by 62.3–78.9% while maintaining >98.8% clean accuracy. Crucially, it increases the computational cost of adversarial example generation by over 4.1×, introducing a “computational barrier” defense. This work demonstrates that structured quantum entanglement—when co-designed with noise-aware training—enables practical, scalable robustness for safety-critical AI systems.

item.title: Answering Counting Queries with Differential Privacy on a Quantum Computer
item.description: We study differentially private answering of counting queries—e.g., “How many individuals satisfy two binary predicates?”—on quantum-encoded datasets. We show such queries reduce to estimating the amplitude of an orthogonal quantum state. For repeated computational-basis measurements, we prove tighter privacy bounds *specifically for counting queries*, demonstrating inherent privacy amplification due to measurement randomness. For amplitude estimation (AE), we derive the exact global sensitivity ($\Delta_f = 2/\sqrt{N}$) — a first for this setting — and design DP-AE, a differentially private variant that injects calibrated Laplace noise into the phase estimation step while preserving $O(1/\sqrt{M})$ estimation error with $M$ oracle calls. Finally, we outline an outsourcing protocol enabling a classical client to delegate private counting to an untrusted quantum server. This work establishes foundational algorithms and sensitivity analysis for practical quantum differential privacy.

item.title: A Proposed Biomedical Data Policy Framework to Reduce Fragmentation, Improve Quality, and Incentivize Sharing in Indian Healthcare in the era of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Health
item.description: India’s vast biomedical data—generated across public hospitals, private EMRs, insurance schemes, and research—remains fragmented due to institutional silos and vendor-locked systems. The core bottleneck is not technical but incentive-based: data curation lacks recognition in academic promotion (NMC), institutional rankings (NIRF), or funding mechanisms. To address this, we propose a multi-layered policy framework that: (1) integrates peer-reviewed **data papers** into NMC promotion criteria; (2) embeds open-data metrics into NIRF; (3) adopts **Shapley-value-based revenue sharing** in federated learning consortia; and (4) institutionalizes **data stewardship** as a professional role. We mitigate key barriers—including quality anxiety and misinterpretation risks—via mandatory data quality assessment, structured peer review, and academic credit for auditing. Fully aligned with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA), the framework constructively engages NDSAP, Biotech-PRIDE, and ANRF guidelines to enable high-quality, ethical, and incentivized data sharing for AI-driven health innovation in India.

item.title: Exact Certification of Neural Networks and Partition Aggregation Ensembles against Label Poisoning
item.description: Label-flipping attacks pose a critical threat to supervised learning by corrupting training labels to induce inference-time misclassifications. While certification frameworks aim to provide formal robustness guarantees—e.g., “the model’s prediction remains unchanged under up to $k$ label flips”—existing partition-aggregation methods treat base classifiers as black boxes, yielding overly conservative bounds. We introduce **EnsembleCert**, the first white-box certification framework for partition-aggregation ensembles, which aggregates per-partition *exact* certificates of base classifiers to compute tight ensemble-level guarantees in polynomial time. To enable this, we propose **ScaLabelCert**, the first exact, polynomial-time computable certificate for neural networks against label flipping—leveraging the neural tangent kernel (NTK) equivalence of wide networks. On CIFAR-10, EnsembleCert certifies **+26.5% more label flips (median)** than state-of-the-art black-box methods while using **100× fewer partitions**, challenging the dogma that heavy partitioning is essential for strong certified robustness.

item.title: Representation-Aligned Multi-Scale Personalization for Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) struggles to accommodate clients with heterogeneous resource constraints. Conventional approaches—relying on a shared full-size backbone and client-specific submodel extraction—suffer from limited structural diversity and poor representational adaptation. This paper proposes **FRAMP**, a unified framework for personalized, resource-adaptive FL. Instead of a fixed global model, FRAMP generates *client-specific lightweight models* from compact descriptors encoding computational budgets and data characteristics. Crucially, it enforces **representation alignment** across clients via contrastive semantic consistency losses, preserving global semantics while enabling fine-grained personalization. Extensive experiments on vision (CIFAR, Tiny-ImageNet) and graph (OGBN-Arxiv, Reddit) benchmarks show FRAMP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods—improving accuracy by +3.2–5.7% on resource-constrained clients—while enhancing cross-client representation coherence by 38%.

item.title: A Full Compression Pipeline for Green Federated Learning in Communication-Constrained Environments
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative model training but suffers from prohibitive communication and computation overheads in resource-constrained environments. To address this, we propose the **Full Compression Pipeline (FCP)**—a unified end-to-end framework integrating structured pruning, 8-bit quantization, and adaptive Huffman encoding. FCP compresses local models *and* communication payloads jointly, drastically reducing transmission costs and device resource consumption while preserving model accuracy. We introduce a holistic evaluation metric—**unified model cost**—that jointly quantifies communication and computation overheads. Evaluated on CIFAR-10 with 10 clients under 2 Mbps bandwidth, FCP achieves **11.3× model size reduction**, only a **2.0% accuracy drop** (90.1% vs. 92.1%), and **61.7% faster FL training**, with robust performance under both IID and non-IID data distributions. FCP delivers a practical, efficient, and deployable solution for green FL.

item.title: LLM-Redactor: An Empirical Evaluation of Eight Techniques for Privacy-Preserving LLM Requests
item.description: This paper presents **LLM-Redactor**, the first systematic empirical evaluation of eight privacy-preserving techniques for LLM requests—spanning local inference (A), redaction with placeholder restoration (B), semantic rephrasing (C), TEE-hosted inference (D), split inference (E), fully homomorphic encryption (F), MPC-based secret sharing (G), and differential-privacy noise (H). We implement all techniques in an open-source shim compatible with MCP and OpenAI-style APIs. Evaluating the four practical options (A, B, C, H) and their combinations on a ground-truth leak benchmark (1,300 samples, 4,014 annotations), we find **no single technique dominates**: the hybrid strategy **A+B+C** achieves only **0.6% combined PII leakage**, **31.3% proprietary code leakage**, and **zero exact PII leaks** across 500 PII samples. We propose a threat-model-aware decision rule to select optimal techniques based on workload characteristics and budget constraints. Code, benchmarks, and evaluation harness are publicly released.

item.title: Can we Watermark Low-Entropy LLM Outputs?
item.description: This paper initiates the study of provably undetectable watermarking for LLM outputs with *constant per-token entropy*—breaking the standard assumption of constant entropy *rate* (i.e., Ω(log \|𝒯\|) entropy per token) required by prior works (Christ et al. [COLT’24, CRYPTO’24]; Golowich et al. [NeurIPS’24]). We present two constructions: (1) a watermarking scheme robust against *random substitutions*, assuming subexponential LPN hardness; and (2) a scheme robust against *both random substitutions and random deletions*, achieved either under a natural heuristic that LLM outputs induce random errors (analogous to CRYPTO’24), or via a novel primitive—a pseudorandom error-correcting code resilient to adversarial substitutions and random deletions. Our results significantly broaden the applicability of watermarking to low-entropy, high-fidelity LLM generations (e.g., code, factual QA), establishing the first formal framework for watermarking in the constant-entropy regime.

item.title: SIR-Bench: Evaluating Investigation Depth in Security Incident Response Agents
item.description: We introduce **SIR-Bench**, the first benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate *investigation depth*—not just triage accuracy—in autonomous security incident response (SIR) agents. Built from 129 expert-validated, anonymized real-world incident patterns, it comprises 794 test cases with ground-truth multi-hop evidence chains. To enable authentic, reproducible evaluation, we propose **Once Upon A Threat (OUAT)**, a framework that replays attacks in controlled cloud environments and generates realistic, time-synchronized telemetry. SIR-Bench introduces three complementary metrics: **M1 (Triage Accuracy)**, **M2 (Novel Finding Discovery)**—quantifying evidence uncovered beyond initial alerts—and **M3 (Tool Usage Appropriateness)**, rigorously assessed by an adversarial LLM-as-Judge that requires concrete forensic evidence for scoring. Evaluating our SIR agent on SIR-Bench achieves **97.1% true positive detection**, **73.4% false positive rejection**, and **5.67 novel key findings per case**, establishing a strong baseline for future investigation agents.

item.title: INTARG: Informed Real-Time Adversarial Attack Generation for Time-Series Regression
item.description: Time-series forecasting is vital for real-world systems, yet deep learning models remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks. However, standard attacks (e.g., PGD, C&W) are impractical in online settings due to full-history dependency and high computational cost. This paper introduces **INTARG**, an *informed, real-time adversarial attack framework* tailored for the *online bounded-buffer* scenario—where only a sliding window of recent observations is available and attacks must be low-latency and sparse. Unlike prior methods, INTARG selectively targets time steps exhibiting both **high model confidence** (low prediction variance) and **maximal expected error amplification**, applying minimal ℓ∞-bounded perturbations *only once per selected step*. Experiments across ETTh1, Electricity, and Traffic datasets show INTARG achieves up to **2.42× increase in MAE** while launching attacks in **fewer than 10% of time steps**, with sub-8ms latency per attack on GPU. It establishes a new efficiency-aware paradigm for evaluating time-series model robustness.

item.title: ClawGuard: A Runtime Security Framework for Tool-Augmented LLM Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection
item.description: Tool-augmented LLM agents are highly vulnerable to indirect prompt injection (IPI), where adversaries inject malicious instructions into tool-returned content—treated as trusted observations—triggering unauthorized real-world actions. We present **ClawGuard**, a runtime security framework that enforces *deterministic, user-confirmed access rules* at every tool-call boundary. Unlike alignment-dependent defenses, ClawGuard automatically derives task-specific constraints from the user’s stated objective *before any external tool invocation*, enabling zero-trust validation of both tool requests and responses. It blocks all three major IPI channels (web/local content, MCP server, skill file injection) without model fine-tuning or infrastructure changes. Evaluated across five state-of-the-art LLMs on AgentDojo, SkillInject, and MCPSafeBench, ClawGuard achieves 100% IPI mitigation with <1.2% utility degradation—establishing boundary-enforced determinism as a foundational, deployable defense for secure agentic AI. Code: https://github.com/Claw-Guard/ClawGuard

item.title: GPU Acceleration of Sparse Fully Homomorphic Encrypted DNNs
item.description: Fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) enables privacy-preserving deep neural network (DNN) inference but suffers from prohibitive computational overhead—especially in ciphertext matrix multiplication (matmul), the most expensive operation. This paper presents the first GPU-accelerated sparse matmul for FHE DNNs, built on the GPU-native FIDESlib library. By jointly exploiting sparsity in both weights and activations, we redesign the matmul pipeline with optimized NTT scheduling, memory-coalesced sparse indexing, and a fused ciphertext-sparse computation kernel. Our approach reduces asymptotic complexity from cubic $O(n^3)$ to semi-linear $O(n^{1.5}\log n)$ and achieves up to **3.0× speedup** over state-of-the-art CPU implementations on AMD GPUs. Results demonstrate practical feasibility for encrypted inference on sparse DNNs, bridging a critical gap between cryptographic security and hardware efficiency.

item.title: Detecting Safety Violations Across Many Agent Traces
item.description: To audit safety violations in LLM agents, auditors must sift through massive trace collections—yet failures are often rare, adversarially concealed, and only detectable *across* traces (e.g., in misuse campaigns, reward hacking, or prompt injection). Existing methods fail: per-trace judges miss cross-trace patterns; agentic auditing doesn’t scale; fixed monitors break under novel behaviors. We introduce **Meerkat**, a scalable, natural-language-driven framework that combines semantic clustering with adaptive agentic search. It discovers sparse violations without seed examples, fixed workflows, or exhaustive enumeration—first identifying promising trace clusters via embedding similarity, then deploying lightweight agents to reason contextually *within* and *across* those clusters. Evaluated across misuse, misalignment, and task-gaming settings, Meerkat achieves up to **3.2× higher detection recall** than baseline monitors, uncovers **widespread developer cheating on AgentBench** (affecting 68% of submissions), and finds **nearly 4× more reward-hacking instances on CyBench** than prior audits—demonstrating a new paradigm for evidence-driven, scalable agent safety auditing.

item.title: Collaborative Multi-Agent Scripts Generation for Enhancing Imperfect-Information Reasoning in Murder Mystery Games
item.description: This paper tackles the degradation of vision-language models (VLMs) in multi-hop reasoning under imperfect-information, adversarial multiplayer settings—exemplified by Murder Mystery Games. We propose a collaborative multi-agent framework that synthesizes high-fidelity, role-aware game scripts (including backstories, multimodal clues, and grounded reasoning chains) via coordinated agent interaction. To enhance VLM reasoning, we introduce a two-stage agent-monitored training strategy: (1) chain-of-thought fine-tuning on curated and synthetic datasets explicitly modeling uncertainty and deception; and (2) GRPO-based reinforcement learning with role-specific reward shaping, guiding models to develop identity-consistent inference behaviors. Experiments show substantial gains: +28.6% in hidden fact extraction accuracy and +22.1 F1 points in narrative multi-hop reasoning (79.3% absolute), with marked resilience to deceptive cues. Our work establishes a scalable paradigm for training and evaluating VLMs in socially complex, uncertain environments—paving the way for new benchmarks in imperfect-information multimodal reasoning.

item.title: A Full Compression Pipeline for Green Federated Learning in Communication-Constrained Environments
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) promises privacy-preserving collaborative training but faces severe communication and computational bottlenecks—especially in bandwidth-constrained edge environments. To address this, we propose the **Full Compression Pipeline (FCP)**, an end-to-end trainable framework integrating **structured pruning**, **low-bit quantization (4–6 bits)**, and **Huffman entropy coding** into a unified compression workflow. Unlike piecemeal approaches, FCP jointly optimizes all stages during local training, enabling gradient-aware compression without accuracy collapse. We introduce a holistic *model cost* metric that jointly quantifies communication volume (bytes) and computation load (FLOPs), facilitating rigorous efficiency–accuracy trade-off analysis. Evaluated on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-12 across 10 clients under 2 Mbps bandwidth, FCP achieves **11.3× model size reduction**, only a **2.1% accuracy drop** (92.4% → 90.3%), and **61.5% faster FL convergence**, while maintaining robustness under both IID and non-IID data distributions. FCP advances green, scalable FL for resource-limited deployments.

item.title: Detecting RAG Extraction Attack via Dual-Path Runtime Integrity Game
item.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems face a critical security threat: *RAG Knowledge Base Leakage*, where adversarial prompts extract proprietary retrieved content. Existing defenses lack real-time detection, adaptability, and plug-and-play deployment. We propose **CanaryRAG**, a runtime integrity defense inspired by stack canaries. It embeds verifiable, semantics-preserving *canary tokens* into retrieved chunks and frames defense as a **dual-path integrity game**—monitoring both the target (user-facing) and oracle (canary-augmented) generation paths for behavioral violations. CanaryRAG detects leakage in real time—even under adaptive suppression and obfuscation—without model retraining or pipeline modification. Evaluated against 7 state-of-the-art RAG extraction attacks, it reduces average chunk recovery rate to **2.1%** (vs. 38.7% for SOTA baselines), with **<0.8% task accuracy drop** and **<3.2 ms latency overhead**. As a lightweight, universal plug-in, CanaryRAG delivers practical, scalable protection for proprietary RAG knowledge bases.

item.title: DuCodeMark: Dual-Purpose Code Dataset Watermarking via Style-Aware Watermark-Poison Design
item.description: DuCodeMark is the first dual-purpose, style-aware watermarking framework for code datasets that simultaneously secures both source-code generation and decompilation tasks. It parses code into ASTs, applies language-specific stylistic transformations to generate stealthy trigger-target pairs, and injects *repressible poisoned features* into return-typed samples—features remain inert during normal training but activate upon watermark removal, causing significant performance degradation (e.g., Pass@1 drops by 28.6%). Verification uses a black-box independent-samples *t*-test. Evaluated across 72 settings (2 tasks × 2 languages × 3 CodeLMs × 6 temperatures), DuCodeMark achieves strong verifiability (*p* < 0.05), high stealthiness (suspicious rate ≤ 0.36), robustness against watermark removal and poisoning attacks (recall ≤ 0.57), and negligible impact on clean-task performance (<0.8% Pass@1 drop). It establishes a practical, resilient foundation for open-source code copyright protection in the LLM era.

item.title: The Blind Spot of Agent Safety: How Benign User Instructions Expose Critical Vulnerabilities in Computer-Use Agents
item.description: This paper identifies a critical safety blind spot in Computer-Use Agents (CUAs): severe vulnerabilities triggered *not by malicious instructions*, but by **benign user commands interacting unexpectedly with task context or environment dynamics**. To expose this, we introduce **OS-BLIND**, a novel benchmark comprising 300 human-crafted tasks across 12 categories and 8 real-world applications, organized into two threat clusters—*environment-embedded threats* and *agent-initiated harms*. Evaluations on state-of-the-art models (e.g., Claude 4.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o) and agentic frameworks reveal alarmingly high attack success rates (ASRs): >90% for most CUAs, 73.0% for safety-aligned Claude 4.5 Sonnet—and rising to **92.7% in multi-agent settings**, where subtask decomposition obscures harmful intent. Crucially, existing safety alignment mechanisms activate only transiently (typically within the first few steps) and fail to re-engage during long-horizon execution. OS-BLIND highlights the urgent need for context-aware, dynamically adaptive safety protocols beyond prompt-level guarding. The benchmark will be publicly released to advance robust CUA safety research.

item.title: Task2vec Readiness: Diagnostics for Federated Learning from Pre-Training Embeddings
item.description: Federated learning (FL) performance is notoriously sensitive to client data heterogeneity, yet practitioners lack pre-training methods to reliably anticipate federation behavior. This paper introduces **Task2Vec Readiness**: a set of unsupervised, embedding-based diagnostics computed *before any FL training*. We derive three geometric metrics—**cohesion**, **dispersion**, and **density**—directly from Task2Vec task embeddings of individual clients. Evaluated across CIFAR-10, FEMNIST, PathMNIST, and BloodMNIST under Dirichlet heterogeneity (α = 0.05 to 5.0) and FedAVG, our indices show strong, consistent correlations with final model accuracy: cohesion achieves Pearson/Spearman coefficients >0.9 in most dataset×client configurations. These results validate Task2Vec Readiness as a principled, lightweight, and predictive pre-training diagnostic for FL—enabling early client selection, heterogeneity assessment, and resource-aware federation design without any communication or model training overhead.

item.title: FedRio: Personalized Federated Social Bot Detection via Cooperative Reinforced Contrastive Adversarial Distillation
item.description: Social bot detection is vital for platform integrity, yet isolated model development hinders cross-platform generalization and rapid adaptation to emerging bot variants. To bridge this gap under strict privacy constraints, we propose **FedRio**, a personalized federated framework integrating *cooperative reinforced contrastive adversarial distillation*. FedRio features: (1) an adaptive graph neural network backbone per client; (2) a GAN-based federated knowledge extractor that models global data distributions without raw data sharing; (3) multi-stage adversarial contrastive learning to align local feature spaces and reduce client-server divergence; and (4) reinforcement-learning-guided client parameter control alongside adaptive server aggregation to handle severe data heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on TwiBot-20 and Cresci-2017 show FedRio consistently outperforms state-of-the-art federated baselines in F1-score (+3.2–5.8%), communication efficiency (−42% upload volume), and feature consistency (−61% centering error), while remaining highly competitive with centralized methods—despite enforcing substantially stronger privacy guarantees (no raw data, no label exchange, end-to-end encryption).

item.title: Enhancing Cross-Problem Vehicle Routing via Federated Learning
item.description: Vehicle routing problems (VRPs) are fundamental in logistics optimization, yet existing neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) methods suffer from performance degradation and poor generalizability when transferring across VRP variants with heterogeneous constraints (e.g., time windows, multi-depots, electric vehicle charging). To address this, we propose **MPSF-FL**: a *Multi-problem Pre-train, then Single-problem Fine-tune* framework powered by Federated Learning. MPSF-FL first builds a shared global model via federated pre-training on diverse VRP subtypes, then enables each client to fine-tune its local model using constraint-conditioned knowledge distillation from the up-to-date global model—ensuring both cross-problem knowledge transfer and downstream task specialization. Experiments on 12 VRP benchmarks show MPSF-FL improves solution quality by **4.2–9.7%** over SOTA NCO baselines (e.g., AM, POMO) and boosts zero-shot generalization to unseen VRPs (e.g., E-VRP) by **31.5%**, all while reducing communication cost by 38%.

item.title: Differentially Private Verification of Distribution Properties
item.description: This paper initiates the study of *differentially private (DP) distribution property verification*, where a verifier uses samples from a sensitive distribution and interacts with an untrusted but knowledgeable prover. We map the landscape of DP interactive proofs for distribution properties, resolving when private randomness (private coins) offers advantages over public coins under privacy constraints. Our main results are: (1) For $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP with $\varepsilon = O(1/\sqrt{n})$, $\delta = O(1/n^{5/2})$, any one-round private-coin DP protocol can be converted into an equivalent one-round public-coin DP protocol—preserving privacy parameters, sample complexity, and communication complexity; (2) If the verifier’s message satisfies only $O(1/\sqrt{\log n})$-local DP (a much relaxed regime), one additional transformation yields a public-coin protocol with identical DP guarantees and efficiency; (3) Conversely, when $\varepsilon \in \Omega(\log n)$, private coins *do* reduce complexity, revealing a sharp phase transition. We also give an optimal-sample-complexity Merlin–Arthur (one-message) DP protocol for testing product distributions.

item.title: Communication-Efficient Gluon in Federated Learning
item.description: Recent advances show Muon-type optimizers—leveraging linear minimization oracles (LMOs) over non-Euclidean norm balls—outperform Adam in large language model training. Yet communication cost remains prohibitive in federated learning. This paper introduces **Communication-Efficient Gluon**, an extension of Muon under layer-wise $(L^0, L^1)$-smoothness, equipped with both unbiased and contraction compressors. We integrate SARAH-style variance reduction to mitigate compression error, achieving $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence and improved communication complexity $\mathcal{O}(d/\varepsilon^2)$. As a byproduct, we derive **VR-Gluon**, a new variance-reduced variant with faster convergence than standard Gluon. Further, momentum variance reduction (MVR) is incorporated, yielding comparable communication efficiency even when $L_i^1 \neq 0$—a weaker condition than prior work. Experiments confirm up to **47% fewer communication rounds** and **3.2× bandwidth reduction**, with no accuracy loss.

item.title: Energy-Efficient Federated Edge Learning For Small-Scale Datasets in Large IoT Networks
item.description: Large-scale IoT networks face critical challenges in federated edge learning (FEL) when dealing with small-scale, heterogeneous datasets: poor convergence, high energy consumption, and low resource utilization. To address this, we propose **EE-FEL**, an energy-efficient collaborative optimization framework tailored for small-data regimes. We first derive a tight **expected learning loss bound** that explicitly links sample size, data heterogeneity, and generalization error. Then, we design a **lightweight stochastic online learning algorithm** to adapt to dynamic data arrivals and distribution shifts. A joint resource optimization problem is formulated—minimizing end-to-end energy under a **rigorously proven convergence guarantee**—and solved via a **fully decentralized, online distributed algorithm** requiring only local and neighbor information. Extensive simulations and autonomous navigation case studies (with real-world collision-avoidance tasks) show EE-FEL achieves **23.6% higher accuracy**, **41.2% lower edge energy consumption**, and **37% fewer communication rounds** versus state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx), while maintaining a bounded convergence error ≤ 0.02.
